content_html:
LLM Use in the Python Source Code

Posted by

on under

There is a trick that is spreading through social media. If you block the claude user on GitHub, then each time you visit a GitHub repository that has commits by this user you get a banner at the top alerting you of the user's participation. It's an easy way to spot projects that have started to rely on coding agents, in this case on Claude Code specifically.

Imagine the surprise when you see that CPython, one of the most popular open-source projects in the world, is now receiving contributions from 

claude
:

What does this mean, exactly?

A repository having the 

claude
user flagged in this way means that the project has at least one commit in which this user appears as author or co-author.

In accurate terms, it means that one or more commits in the project have this or something similar in the commit message:

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

This could happen if a developer working on the Python source code allows Claude Code to commit code changes on its own to their local clone of the Python git repository. Eventually the developer pushes whatever work they were doing with Claude Code assistance to the CPython project on GitHub, and here the 

claude
user goes along with it, appearing as a co-author with the developer in the pushed commit.

What we cannot tell is which specific lines in a commit were generated by Anthropic's coding tool. Also we cannot tell if there are other commits in the Python source code that were generated by coding assistants but were manually committed by the developer under their name. The only thing we can tell with certainty is that the CPython project is allowing developers to use LLMs, either explicitly or implicitly by not having a clear policy that forbids it.

So far there are only 8 commits with user 

claude
as co-author, made over the last six months or so. They cover a small portion of the code, so there is no reason to panic just yet.

How I feel about this

This rubs me the wrong way on several levels. I can't say that I'm surprised, but yeah, it is a big disappointment.

Of course there are the environmental, ethical, moral and even legal issues associated with the use of LLMs. I do not see value in bringing up these arguments here, simply because they do not seem to be effective at persuading people to stop using these tools. What I'll do is mention a couple of additional reasons that concern me about this particular situation.

First of all, I feel allowing developers to use LLMs or coding assistants in their contributions to CPython robs people in the Python community from the opportunity of making such contributions themselves, and learning from the experience. I can't really imagine that CPython is having issues finding contributors, so why choose to generate code with an LLM instead of giving access to people willing to help out, become involved and grow as developers?

I also don't really agree with the idea that code that was generated with an LLM coding tool should be attributed, in whole or in part, to the tool itself. The fact that Claude Code "signs" its commits with a made-up user feels ridiculous to me. In my opinion, the developer that is driving the tool should assume full ownership and responsibility for the code that is generated by a coding tool under their supervision.

Now don't get me wrong, I think it is a good idea for code that was generated with LLMs to be flagged in some way, but giving attribution of the code to an entity that does not exist is not the way. I hope CPython stops allowing this. And if they decide to keep accepting LLM-generated code in the future, then they should find a proper way to identify such contributions without altering ownership. They should also figure out a solution that applies to all the tools of this type and not just Claude Code.

What I would really like to ask of the CPython project is transparency. Do you allow core developers to use LLMs for coding? What about regular contributors? For which kinds of tasks? Are there any tasks for which coding assistance is not allowed? Do you require contributors to declare if their contributions were made with the help of LLMs?

I have many more questions. I hope they share more information with regard to their use of LLMs and coding agents going forward.

Update: Hugo van Kemenade sent a link to the Generative AI page in the Python Developer's Guide. I had seen this, but it did not seem to be a "policy" but more an informational page. In any case, the "Acceptable uses" section does not mention contributing code to CPython, and the "Unaceptable uses" section does not clearly forbid it, so it remains vague, in my opinion. There are indirect references to contributions made with LLMs in the "Considerations for success" section though.

Buy me a coffee?

Thank you for visiting my blog! If you enjoyed this article, please consider supporting my work and keeping me caffeinated with a small one-time donation through Buy me a coffee. Thanks!

Share this post

  • #1 Marco Nero said

    Most people manage to use code assistants for six months without summoning apocalyptic horsemen or violating the Geneva Conventions. Apparently not everyone can resist turning “I let Claude write a docstring” into a moral crisis requiring blogposts and hand-wringing.

  • #2 Miguel Grinberg said

    @Marco: thanks for your opinion. "Most people" do not work on a project that is as influential as CPython, so whatever you think these "most people" do with code assistants is irrelevant here. The CPython folks have a responsibility to the Python community and should lead by example. Imagine what new developers who can't afford to pay $200/month for a chatbot will feel when they see that the CPython project has become dependent on this technology, which by the way, is proprietary and as far from open source ideals as it can be.

  • #3 Shwetank said

    That 'committed by claude' can be easily turned off.

    I think the actual amount of code vibe coded with LLM is a lot more than 8 commits.

  • #4 Miguel Grinberg said

    @Shwetank: Yes, I do indicate in the article that having Claude as a co-author is a matter of choice and its absence is not indicative of anything. Nobody talked to me on the record about the use of Claude Code by CPython core developers. But yes, based on what I unofficially heard, I also feel that this is bigger than 8 commits and one core developer.

  • #5 Álvaro said

    1957-> Page 2 -> Introduction -> third phrase:

    In a few years, all code will be written by LLMs, it will be less efficient, and we will lose—or pass on—that knowledge to machines… and everything will go wrong… just as it has been happening since the first Homo sapiens decided to delegate tasks to tools in order to progress. It’s in our nature, and I’m not saying whether that’s better or worse—those are subjective terms.

title: LLM Use in the Python Source Code
